skill.name: service-mesh-observability
skill.description: Plan service mesh observability with tracing, metrics, dashboards, alerts, and troubleshooting workflows for Istio, Linkerd, Kiali, Jaeger, and OpenTelemetry.
skill.summary: Build and troubleshoot service mesh observability for Kubernetes platforms.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, PromQL expressions, and documentation links. Real publish concerns remain in example manifests that enable anonymous Kiali access, wildcard namespace visibility, broad listener bindings, and insecure telemetry TLS settings.

medium_findings.title: Anonymous Kiali Access Across All Namespaces
medium_findings.description: The Kiali example sets anonymous authentication and wildcard namespace access. If copied into a shared cluster, it can expose mesh topology and service metadata too broadly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The configuration explicitly uses anonymous access and accessible_namespaces set to a wildcard. This is a clear copy-paste security risk, although it is documentation and not executed by the skill.

medium_findings.title: Insecure Telemetry Collector Example
medium_findings.description: The OpenTelemetry example binds receivers and a Prometheus exporter to 0.0.0.0 and disables TLS verification for the Jaeger exporter.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and tls.insecure settings are visible in the example. The risk depends on deployment context, so the finding is medium rather than high.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported external command locations are Markdown code fences or inline resource references. The skill contains documentation, not Ruby code that executes shell backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows Markdown fences and one bash example block. No Ruby runtime, interpolation, or automatic command execution path is present.

low_findings.title: Documented Cluster Commands Require Operator Review
low_findings.description: The Linkerd section includes shell commands that install or query mesh observability components. They are legitimate examples but can alter a Kubernetes cluster if copied.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and relevant to the skill purpose. They are not hidden or malicious, but they deserve user review before execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts Match Query Syntax
low_findings.description: No weak cryptographic algorithm usage was found. The alerts map to service mesh text, PromQL rate windows, histogram queries, and alert annotations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain observability descriptions and query expressions, not crypto APIs or hashing functions. The scanner appears to match short substrings in normal text.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Path Traversal Alert in PromQL Regex
low_findings.description: The path traversal alert is caused by a response-code regular expression in a Grafana query. It is not a filesystem path or file access operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a PromQL expression matching 5xx response codes. There is no path handling, file read, or filesystem write behavior nearby.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Internal Service Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to in-cluster observability services and public documentation. They do not send secrets or perform outbound exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in YAML examples and resource links. No code path collects local data or transmits credentials to those endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Anonymous Dashboard Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The Kiali template demonstrates anonymous dashboard access with all namespaces visible. Users should replace it with token or OpenID authentication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky settings are explicit and easy to copy. The issue is contextual because the skill is documentation only.

dangerous_patterns.title: Insecure TLS and Broad Bind Addresses
dangerous_patterns.description: The OpenTelemetry template uses 0.0.0.0 listener addresses and tls.insecure. Users should restrict listeners and enable TLS validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The configuration is visible in the collector example. Exposure depends on network policy and deployment boundaries.

content.value_statement: Service mesh failures are hard to trace across many services. This skill gives practical templates for metrics, traces, dashboards, and alerts.
actual_capabilities: Explains core service mesh observability signals for latency, traffic, errors, and saturation.
Provides Istio metrics, tracing, and Prometheus configuration examples.
Lists Linkerd viz commands for dashboard access, routes, tap, and service edges.
Shows Kiali, Jaeger, Grafana, and OpenTelemetry integration patterns.
Defines alert examples for error rate, latency, and certificate expiration.
Gives practical guidance for sampling, retention, dashboards, and label cardinality.
limitations: It is a documentation-only skill and does not deploy components automatically.
Examples must be adapted for production security, namespaces, and network policy.
It does not validate live cluster state or check installed mesh versions.
Some image versions and dashboard formats may need updates for newer platforms.

use_cases.title: Set Up Mesh Dashboards
use_cases.description: Create a starting observability stack for Istio or Linkerd using Prometheus, Grafana, Kiali, and tracing tools.

use_cases.title: Investigate Latency Spikes
use_cases.description: Use golden signals, route metrics, traces, and topology views to isolate slow service calls.

use_cases.title: Define Service Communication SLOs
use_cases.description: Convert mesh metrics into alert rules and dashboards for latency, error rate, and certificate health.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Observability Signals
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new mesh.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose service mesh observability signals for an Istio cluster with ten services. Include dashboards and alerts.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Istio Monitoring Stack
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an Istio observability setup with Prometheus, Grafana, Jaeger, and Kiali. Explain required components and safe production changes.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot Mesh Latency
prompt_templates.prompt: I have high P99 latency between frontend and checkout services. Build a troubleshooting plan using metrics, traces, and mesh topology.

prompt_templates.title: Harden Telemetry Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my service mesh observability design for production security. Focus on authentication, namespace scope, TLS, sampling, and cardinality.

output_examples.input: Plan observability for a new Istio mesh.
output_examples.output: A phased setup plan covering Prometheus scraping, Grafana dashboards, Jaeger tracing, Kiali topology, sampling policy, and production hardening notes.

output_examples.input: Debug rising checkout latency in Linkerd.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist using route metrics, live traffic inspection, service edges, trace correlation, and alert threshold review.

output_examples.input: Create SLO alerts for service communication.
output_examples.output: A concise alert strategy for error rate, P99 latency, certificate expiration, severity labels, and dashboard ownership.

best_practices: Require authenticated dashboard access and limit namespace visibility before production use.
Tune trace sampling for each environment to balance detail, privacy, and cost.
Control metric cardinality by limiting high-cardinality labels and route dimensions.
anti_patterns: Do not copy anonymous dashboard settings into shared or production clusters.
Do not expose telemetry receivers broadly without network policy and TLS validation.
Do not alert on raw request volume without service ownership and error budget context.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy service mesh tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that an operator must review and adapt.

faq.question: Which meshes does it cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on Istio and Linkerd, with related examples for Kiali, Jaeger, Prometheus, Grafana, and OpenTelemetry.

faq.question: Can I use the examples in production?
faq.answer: Yes, after hardening authentication, namespace scope, TLS, sampling, versions, and network exposure.

faq.question: Does it include alerting guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes alert patterns for error rate, P99 latency, and certificate expiration.

faq.question: Will it analyze my live cluster?
faq.answer: No. It can help plan commands and checks, but it does not inspect cluster state by itself.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation-only, but some examples show insecure settings that need production changes.
